notice: ⚠️ This is an unofficial community site and is not the official Komaki City website. For official information, please visit the Komaki City Board of Education.
footer_title: Komaki East School Reorganization – Community Info Site
footer_tagline: A volunteer community site providing easy-to-understand information on the Shinooka school reorganization plan, based on official Komaki City sources.
footer_copyright: © 2026 Komaki East School Reorganization Community Info Site (Unofficial) | This site is operated by community volunteers and is not affiliated with Komaki City or its Board of Education.
hero_title:
Komaki City East (Shinooka Area)
School Reorganization Plan
hero_sub: Easy-to-understand information on the reorganization of elementary and junior high schools in the Shinooka area of eastern Komaki City, based on official sources.
section_whatis: What is the Shinooka School Reorganization Plan?<small>About the Reorganization Plan</small>
about_p: Detailed information on the Shinooka school reorganization plan — background, current situation, target schools, and commuting arrangements — based on official Komaki City sources.
schedule_p: Key events from 2025 and the overall roadmap toward the school reorganization in April 2027.
voices_p: A balanced overview of perspectives from local residents, parents, former teachers, and others.
faq_p: Common questions about the reorganization plan and answers based on official city responses. Click to expand each answer.
map_p: View the locations of current schools and planned new school sites in the Shinooka area.
council_p: A summary of the ordinance amendment, committee review, general questions, and petitions at the 1st Regular Council Session of 2026.
community_p: What are community councils in Komaki's Shinooka area? How are they tied to local schools, and what changes — or stays the same — after reorganization?
comm_sec1_h2: Community Councils<small>What Is a Community Council?</small>
comm_sec3_h2: Changes After Reorganization<small>What Changes, What Stays</small>
comm_sec3_intro: The following reflects official city policy. Details are still being finalized.
comm_row1_now: 5 councils in Shinooka area (one per elementary school district)
comm_row2_now: Established at each school (currently covering 8 schools)
comm_row3_now: Community use of each school's facilities
comm_row5_now: Each council hosts its own community-specific events
point1_p: Due to rapid population decline, the number of children in the area has halved over 20 years. By 2033, all elementary schools and Shinooka JHS are projected to have one class per grade — a situation with no time to wait.
point2_p: In FY2027, 5 elementary and 3 junior high schools will be consolidated into 2 of each. Free school bus service (fully city-funded) for eligible-area elementary students, and bicycle commuting for junior high students.
point3_p: In 2025, five sessions of the "Think About Schools" meetings, community briefings, parent opinion exchanges, and a public comment process (55 submissions) were held.
point4_p: Yearly class reshuffles, diverse club activities, and seamless K–9 education — reorganization opens up richer school experiences for every child.
ql_about_p: Detailed reorganization plan, current statistics, school consolidation groupings (the four Shinooka Gakuen schools), and commuting plans.
ql_schedule_p: A record of 2025 community briefings and public comments, plus the roadmap toward implementation in 2027.
ql_voices_p: A fair presentation of diverse opinions from local residents, parents, and former teachers. Includes a summary of the public comment results.
ql_faq_p: Answers questions like "How will commuting work?", "What about bus fees?", and "What happens to the school buildings?" based on official city responses.
ql_council_p: Summarizes the ordinance amendment, committee review, general questions, and petition deliberations at the 1st Regular Council Session of 2026.
ql_bus_p: Free school bus service for elementary students starting FY2027. Fully city-funded. Service areas, schedules, safety measures, and FAQ.
ql_community_p: What role do community councils play in school life? How does the reorganization affect them? What stays the same?
ql_map_p: View the locations of the schools targeted for reorganization and the planned new school sites.
voices_about: About this page: The opinions shown here are summaries of voices gathered through community briefings, opinion exchanges, and public comments, representing pro, con, and neutral perspectives. This page does not endorse any particular stance; it aims to fairly convey the diversity of community views.
pubcom_link: View public comment results on the official city website →
faq_cat1: About the Reorganization Plan<small>About the Plan</small>
cal_nav_hint: Use the ◀▶ buttons to navigate months.
about_why_h2: Why Is Reorganization Needed Now?<small>Why Now?</small>
issue1_p: As of 2025, the population aged 19 and under in the Shinooka area has fallen to less than half compared to 20 years ago. Meanwhile, the population aged 65 and over has increased approximately 2.5 times.
issue2_p: Shinooka Elementary School was built in 1958 (over 60 years ago), and Shinooka Junior High School was built in 1965 (about 60 years ago). Other schools are similarly aging.
issue3_p: If current trends continue, by 2033, all elementary schools and Shinooka Junior High School in the Shinooka area are projected to have only one class per grade.
stat_class_label:
All elementary schools & Shinooka JHS projected
to have 1 class per grade
about_city_view: City's Position: "Given the declining number of students, there is no time to delay school reorganization — it must be carried out as soon as possible." (Komaki City Board of Education)
about_merits_intro: Based on official city documents, a parent survey (July 2025, 471 respondents), and discussions at the School Review Meetings, here are the main expected benefits of the school reorganization.
merit1_p: With one class per grade, students spend all 6 years of elementary school and 3 years of junior high with the same classmates. After reorganization, schools will be large enough to reshuffle classes every year, preventing relationships from becoming rigid. 66.4% of parents cited fixed social dynamics as a current concern.
merit2_p: Daily interaction with more classmates creates an environment where children naturally encounter diverse ways of thinking and values. In the parent survey, 52.2% hoped for opportunities to develop independence and teamwork in a group, while 48.6% wanted an environment with more friends.
merit3_p: More students means more clubs, committees, and activities to choose from. This was the #1 expectation (55.2%) among parents in the survey — the top benefit cited for reorganization.
merit4_p: The four reorganized schools will operate as "Shinooka Gakuen" with a unified 9-year curriculum. Stronger elementary–junior high coordination is expected to ease the "middle school transition gap." 46.1% of parents expect improved academic and life guidance through this coordination.
merit5_p: Small schools struggle to maintain a well-balanced mix of experienced and specialist teachers. Larger schools enable better staffing, allowing diverse teaching expertise to be fully utilized.
merit6_p: Shinooka Elementary School was built in 1958 — now 67 years old; Shinooka Junior High in 1965. Reorganization allows the city to carry out planned replacements and provide students with safe, modern learning environments.
about_merits_survey_box:
📊 Parent Survey (July 2025, 471 responses) — Top expectations for reorganization:
🥇 Wider range of clubs & extracurricular activities 55.2%
🥈 Opportunities to develop independence & teamwork 52.2%
🥉 An environment with more friends 48.6%
📄 Source: Komaki City School Reorganization Official Page
merit_stat2_label: Develop independence & teamwork in a group
about_plan_intro: In FY2027, the current 5 elementary schools and 3 junior high schools will be reorganized into 2 elementary schools and 2 junior high schools. Schools are grouped into "East" (Tokadai Higashi) and "West" (Tokadai Nishi).
about_oshiro_box: About the Oshiro Elementary School District: The Oshiro district is divided into two. The Okusa/Shiroyama 3-chome area will merge into Tokadai Higashi Elementary, while the Shiroyama 2nd, 4th & 5th-chome area will merge into Tokadai Nishi Elementary. For Shiroyama 3rd & 4th-chome, a community meeting was held on July 27, 2025, and a resident survey was conducted before finalizing the district boundaries.
about_mapnote_p: The map below shows the Shinooka area. Click each school name to view its location on Google Maps.
about_map_note: ※ The 4 new schools will use the existing buildings of the same-named schools.
transport_elem_p:
School bus service for eligible elementary students in the service area (commuting distance of 2 km or more, etc.) — fully covered by the city, free for students.

· Morning: 1 trip
· Afternoon: Up to 3 trips (lower grades / upper grades / club days)
· Cost: Free (fully city-funded)

Specific routes and stops are being finalized. → School Bus details page
transport_jhs_p:
Bicycle commuting is planned for junior high students.

Details regarding safe commuting routes and rainy-day arrangements will be studied and announced at a later date.
about_phase2_box: Outlook after Phase 1: If student numbers continue to decline after Phase 1 (FY2027), further consolidation into 1 elementary and 1 junior high school may be considered, along with the introduction of integrated elementary-junior high education. The decision on Phase 2 will be made based on the situation following Phase 1.
about_buildings_h2: About Closed School Facilities<small>Closed School Buildings</small>
building_p: The buildings of closed schools are generally planned to be demolished.
gym_p: Gymnasiums and school grounds are generally planned to be kept. They are expected to be used for local sports and community activities.
map_overview_p: An overview of the Shinooka area (around Tokadai). Click the school name links to view details on Google Maps.
map_before_p: The locations of the current 8 schools serving until March 2027. Merger destinations are shown by East/West group. Click each school name to view its location on Google Maps.
merge_east_jhs: → Merging into Tokadai Higashi Junior High
merge_west_jhs: → Merging into Tokadai Nishi Junior High
map_after_h2: New School Locations<small>New School Locations (from April 2027)</small>
map_after_p: The building locations of the 4 schools starting in April 2027. They will use the existing buildings of the same-named schools (Tokadai Higashi Elementary/JHS and Tokadai Nishi Elementary/JHS). Schools are divided into "East Group" and "West Group."
map_card_east_elem_src: Merging from: Shinooka Elem, Hikarigaoka Elem, Oshiro Elem (Okusa/Shiroyama 3)
map_card_west_elem_src: Merging from: Momogaoka Elem, Sue Elem, Oshiro Elem (Shiroyama 2/4/5)
map_card_east_jhs_src: Merging from: Shinooka JHS, Hikarigaoka JHS (excl. Shiroyama 4)
map_card_west_jhs_src: Merging from: Toryou JHS, Hikarigaoka JHS (Shiroyama 4)
about_multilingual_box: Multilingual Support: The "Shinooka School Reorganization Newsletter" is also available in Spanish, Tagalog, Vietnamese, Portuguese, and Chinese. The city has taken care to provide information to parents with foreign roots.
phase1_body: Held "School Discussion Meetings" (5 sessions total), reported to community councils and neighborhood association heads. Shared the current situation in Shinooka district (rapid decline in student numbers, aging facilities) with local residents.
phase2_body: Conducted student surveys (online), parent surveys (online), Shiroyama 3&4-chome briefings, parent opinion exchange meetings (3 sessions), and a community briefing. Also conducted the new school name survey.
phase3_body: Held public comment on the draft reorganization plan. A total of 55 submissions were received (39 email, 13 fax, 1 mail, 2 in person). The city published responses and a revised plan.
phase4_body: Held the School District Advisory Committee on January 13, 2026. The 5th "School Discussion Meeting" was held on February 7 at the East Community Center.
phase5_body: Official decision on the reorganization plan, formulation of school bus plan, establishment of preparation committees for new schools, etc. Please check the official announcements from Komaki City Board of Education for details.
phase6_body: The 4-school system of Tokadai Higashi Elementary, Tokadai Nishi Elementary, Tokadai Higashi JHS, and Tokadai Nishi JHS will launch. School bus service for elementary students (fully funded by the city — free) will also begin.

sched_desc28:
Phase 1 Reorganization — Launch (Planned)

sched_note: Note: The above schedule is based on official information from Komaki City Board of Education. Please always confirm the latest information and details at the official city page or by contacting the Education General Affairs Division (TEL: 0568-39-5261).
faq_q1: OverviewWhy is the reorganization happening in FY2027?
faq_a1:
The city states: "Given the declining number of students, there is no time to delay school reorganization — it must be carried out as soon as possible."

As of 2025, the population aged 19 and under in the Shinooka area has fallen to less than half compared to 20 years ago. If current trends continue, by 2033 all elementary schools and Shinooka JHS are projected to have only one class per grade, raising concerns about insufficient numbers for group activities, sports events, and club activities.
faq_a2:
In Phase 1 (FY2027), schools will be reorganized as follows:

[Elementary]
· Tokadai Higashi Elementary ← Shinooka, Hikari-ga-oka, Oshiro (Okusa & Shiroyama 3-chome)
· Tokadai Nishi Elementary ← Momo-ga-oka, Sue, Oshiro (Shiroyama 2nd/4th/5th-chome)

[Junior High]
· Tokadai Higashi JHS ← Shinooka JHS, Hikari-ga-oka JHS (except Shiroyama 4-chome)
· Tokadai Nishi JHS ← Tōryō JHS, Hikari-ga-oka JHS (Shiroyama 4-chome)

The Oshiro Elementary district is split depending on the specific neighborhood.
faq_a3: After Phase 1 (FY2027), if student numbers continue to decline, the two elementary schools may be further consolidated into 1 elementary and 1 junior high, introducing integrated elementary-junior high education. The decision on Phase 2 will be based on outcomes of Phase 1.
faq_a4:
School bus service is planned for eligible elementary students in the service area (commuting distance of 2 km or more, etc.) — fully free.

· Morning route: 1 trip
· Afternoon routes: Up to 3 trips (lower grades / upper grades / club days)
· Cost: Fully covered by the city (free for students)

For service area details and stops, see the School Bus page.
faq_q5: CommutingHow will junior high students commute?
faq_a5: Bicycle commuting is planned for junior high students. Details on safe routes and rainy-day arrangements will be studied and announced in due course.
faq_q6: ClubsWhat will happen to extracurricular clubs?
faq_a6: With more students after consolidation, a wider variety of club activities is expected. Currently some clubs cannot run due to low numbers at small schools. After consolidation, students should have more options. Specific clubs will be decided during preparation based on teacher staffing and facilities.
faq_q7: FacilitiesWhat will happen to the buildings of closing schools?
faq_a7:
The school buildings are generally planned to be demolished.
However, gymnasiums and school grounds are generally planned to be kept for local sports and community activities.
faq_q8: CommunityWhat will happen to sports days and community events?
faq_a8: School-run sports days will be held at the consolidated schools. However, community events organized by local neighborhood associations (sports days, summer festivals, etc.) can continue independently. Maintaining links with former school districts' community events after consolidation is a topic for the preparation process.
faq_q9: FacilitiesWhich buildings will the new schools use?
faq_a9: The new schools will use the current buildings of Hikari-ga-oka Elementary, Momo-ga-oka Elementary, Hikari-ga-oka JHS, and Tōryō JHS respectively. Details on renovations will be studied and announced in due course.
faq_q10: ParticipationHas the public comment period ended?
faq_a10:
Yes. The public comment period ran from November 10 to December 9, 2025, receiving 55 submissions (39 email, 13 fax, 1 post, 2 in person).

The city has published its responses and a revised plan on the official city website.
faq_q11: ParticipationCan I still share my opinion with the city?
faq_a11:
While the public comment period has closed, you can still share views by phone or in person at the School Reorganization Promotion Division (TEL: 0568-39-5261).

For more details, visit the official city website.
faq_q12: ParticipationIs information available in other languages?
faq_a12: Yes. Issues 1–4 of the "Shinooka School Reorganization Newsletter" have been translated into Spanish, Filipino (Tagalog), Vietnamese, Portuguese, and Chinese. Download them from the official city website.
faq_contact_dept_val: School Reorganization Promotion Division, Education General Affairs Section, Board of Education
council_source_intro: Source: Based on Komaki City Council Newsletter No. 214 (published May 2026). Please refer to Newsletter No. 214 for accurate official information.
council_overview_p: At the 1st Regular Session held in March 2026, 53 bills were deliberated, including bills and a petition related to school reorganization. The ordinance amendment to implement the reorganization plan was passed by majority vote, though some council members voted against it, making for a session with significant debate.
council_ord_title: 🏫 Amendment to the Komaki City Municipal School Establishment Ordinance
council_ord_desc: This amendment formally establishes in the ordinance the "establishment and abolition of schools" needed to implement the Shinooka reorganization. It establishes Tokadai Higashi Elementary, Tokadai Nishi Elementary, Tokadai Higashi JHS, and Tokadai Nishi JHS, and includes the abolition of schools targeted for reorganization.
council_tl1_text: Referred to the Education and Construction Committee (Subcommittee) for detailed review.
council_tl2_text: Passed by majority (4 in favor, 2 against, 1 absent). Members raised questions about the plan and school bus arrangements (see below).
council_tl3_text: Passed by majority vote. Not unanimous — dissenting votes cast (18 in favor, 6 against, 1 absent)(★ listed in Newsletter as a "split-vote bill").
council_bus_subtitle: Review of "Shinooka District School Bus Operation" (multi-year commitment)
council_bus_desc: After reorganization, some students will have longer commutes, so school bus service is planned. The committee questioned the details of the operating contract.
council_bus_q1: Please explain the school bus operating contract details and the preparation schedule going forward.
council_bus_a1: A pre-enrollment trial will be conducted early in FY2026, with trial bus rides in FY2026–2027. We will prepare carefully, with consideration for all involved, to ensure a smooth full launch.
council_bus_schedule:
School Bus Schedule (from council response)
  • FY2026 (early): Pre-enrollment trial
  • FY2026–2027: Trial rides and test operation
  • FY2027 (April 2027): Full operation begins with Phase 1 reorganization
council_gq_intro: Questions were raised from three angles: the public comment results and information sharing with residents, the specific content and rationale of the reorganization plan, and the city's stance toward community feedback.
council_gq_q1: Please explain the trends and key content of public comment submissions and the city's view. Also, what steps will be taken to share information and build community understanding?
council_gq_a1: We have held multiple briefing sessions and gathered opinions from local residents. We will continue working to deepen understanding of the plan.
council_gq_q2: The reorganization splits school districts in ways that seem to conflict with community realities. Please explain the plan's rationale and whether it truly fits the local situation.
council_gq_a2: This consolidates elementary schools in Shinooka. Due to declining student numbers, we are reorganizing to create an environment where children can learn alongside diverse peers. We need class sizes of around 21–25 students. School buses are planned for students with longer commutes, with broad coverage as most of Shinooka faces distance challenges.
council_gq_q3: (Follow-up) Many public comments called for review or halt of the plan. How do you view this? Have community views been adequately incorporated?
council_gq_a3: We have responded carefully to all submissions. Rather than rushing, we will proceed with full information disclosure. Our mission is to establish a stable educational environment for children as soon as possible.
council_warn_title: ⚠️ Issue Highlighted in General Questions: Public Comment Assessment
council_warn_p: It was pointed out that a high proportion of public comments called for the plan to be "reviewed or halted", raising questions about the adequacy of the city's response. The city replied it had "responded carefully" and would "proceed with full transparency," maintaining its pro-reorganization stance.
council_warn_link: See public comment details and community voices →
council_pet_title: 📝 Petition: "Halt School Reorganization Without Resident Consensus"
council_pet_desc: A petition requesting a halt to the school reorganization being proceeded without community consensus was submitted by residents. Following council deliberation, it was not adopted.
council_pet_meaning: What "not adopted" means: The council did not recognize the petition's intent (halting reorganization). The school reorganization plan will continue to be implemented.
council_s5_h: Summary of Votes on Reorganization Bills<small>Vote Breakdown</small>
council_row1_bill: Amendment to the Municipal School Establishment Ordinance
council_row2_bill: Shinooka District School Bus Operation (multi-year commitment)
council_row3_bill: Petition to halt reorganization without resident consensus
council_vote_note: ★ Listed in the Newsletter as a "split-vote bill" (not unanimously approved). For individual vote details, see the vote record in Council Newsletter No. 214.
council_pt1: The ordinance amendment passed by majority, formally establishing the new schools (Tokadai Higashi/Nishi Elementary & JHS) and abolishing the existing ones.
council_pt2: The committee confirmed the school bus schedule (FY2026 pre-enrollment → trial rides → FY2027 full operation).
council_pt3: General questions focused on responses to public comments and information sharing. Many comments called for review or halt, but the city maintained its stance.
council_pt4: The petition requesting a halt to reorganization was not adopted.
council_pt5: Both the ordinance amendment and petition were decided by non-unanimous votes, reflecting a divided council.
council_ref_box:
Sources & References
This page is a summarized and reorganized version of Komaki City Council Newsletter No. 214 (May 2026) for public accessibility. For member names, detailed votes, and exact statements, please refer to the original. This is an unofficial community site, not affiliated with Komaki City.
pubcom_desc: During the public comment period (November 10 – December 9, 2025), a total of 55 submissions were received. The city has published its responses and a revised plan on the official website.
voice_for1: "If we leave things as they are, by 2033 every school will have just one class per grade. To create an environment where children can learn alongside diverse peers, moving ahead with consolidation now is necessary."
voice_for2: "I was surprised to hear Shinooka Elementary was built in 1958. Sending my child to a 60-year-old building honestly worries me. I support using the reorganization as an opportunity to update the facilities."
voice_for3: "At the current small school, there are few club options and my child had to give up their preferred sport. I'm hopeful that merging into the new schools will open up more choices. The free school bus is also a big help."
voice_for4: "At small schools, teachers often juggle multiple subjects, making specialized instruction difficult. With a properly sized school, teachers can focus on their expertise and lesson quality should improve."
voice_for5: "The population over 65 in the Shinooka area has grown 2.5 times in 20 years while children have halved. There are limits to maintaining multiple small schools. Considering the financial burden on future generations, consolidation is unavoidable."
voice_for6: "As this area has many families with foreign roots, multilingual newsletters are a positive step. An appropriately sized school would bring together children with diverse backgrounds, creating a better educational environment."
voice_against1: "It's dangerous for young children to commute far. Even with a school bus, a long walk to the stop defeats the purpose. Deciding on consolidation before specific safety measures have been presented seems rushed."
voice_against2: "Schools are more than educational facilities — they are the heart of the community. If Shinooka and Momo-ga-oka Elementary disappear, those neighborhoods lose their sports day and festival venues, potentially accelerating community decline."
voice_against3: "Precisely because class sizes are small, teachers can give each child individual attention. The personalized instruction at small schools like Sue Elementary will inevitably be lost as the school grows. Bigger does not equal better education."
voice_against4: "When students suddenly double after consolidation, large-school problems emerge. During my teaching career, I witnessed many times how bullying becomes more complex and children get lost in the crowd."
voice_against5: "Shinooka Junior High has nurtured the children of this community for many years. If the building is old, renovate it. Is it acceptable to close a school with such history so easily? Demolishing the buildings is also deeply sad."
voice_against6: "Currently my child's school is a 5-minute walk, so I can easily drop by as a parent. If the school is far, my involvement will decrease and it will be harder to know what's happening with my child."
voice_against7: "I object to the name 'Shinooka Gakuen.' Using the name of an existing school as the umbrella name for the four new schools feels inconsistent. If the reorganization is a new start, shouldn't the name reflect the diverse history of the broader eastern area?"
voice_neutral1: "I appreciate the city fully covering school bus costs. However, I'd like specific plans — bus stop locations, departure times, bad weather procedures — presented as soon as possible before forming my opinion."
voice_neutral2: "I hope a system can be created so the new schools coordinate with local community events (sports days, summer festivals) in each neighborhood. Even as school structure changes, I hope community bonds are protected."
voice_neutral3: "I'd like the city's responses to the 55 public comments released in a more accessible way. Reading PDFs is difficult for the general public. A plain-language explanation of the revised plan would be appreciated."
voice_neutral4: "Phase 2 envisions consolidating to 1 elementary and 1 junior high with integrated education. Please verify whether Phase 1 truly improves the educational environment before deciding on Phase 2."
voice_neutral5: "Publishing the reorganization newsletter in Spanish, Filipino, Vietnamese, and other languages was a good step. But I hope interpretation at information sessions and multilingual submission options will also be made available."
index_whatis_p1: In the Shinooka area of eastern Komaki City, rapid population decline and aging school facilities have become serious challenges. As of 2025, the population aged 19 and under in the area has fallen to less than half compared to 20 years ago, and if current trends continue, it is projected that by 2033 all elementary schools and Shinooka Junior High School will have only one class per grade.
index_whatis_p2: In response to this situation, Komaki City has formulated this reorganization plan, viewing the entire Shinooka area as a unified learning community to enrich nine years of continuous education. The four post-reorganization schools will be collectively called "Shinooka Gakuen." The plan aims to consolidate the current 5 elementary and 3 junior high schools into 2 elementary and 2 junior high schools in FY2027.
index_whatis_box:
Current Schools (Target for Reorganization):
[Elementary: 5] Shinooka · Hikari-ga-oka · Oshiro · Momo-ga-oka · Sue
[Junior High: 3] Shinooka · Hikari-ga-oka · Toryō

After Reorganization (from FY2027):
[Elementary: 2] Tokadai East Elementary · Tokadai West Elementary
[Junior High: 2] Tokadai East Junior High · Tokadai West Junior High
index_whatis_merits:
Expected benefits of reorganization:

  • Class reshuffles become possible every year
  • Exposure to diverse peers and broader perspectives
  • Wider range of clubs and extracurricular activities
  • Seamless K–9 education with a reduced middle school transition gap
  • Better-balanced teaching staff with diverse expertise
  • Safe learning environments through planned renewal of aging school buildings
status_c1:
May–Oct 2025
Five "Think About Schools" sessions, community briefings, parent opinion exchanges, and surveys held
status_c2:

meta_title_index: Komaki East School Reorganization Plan – Community Info Site (Unofficial)
meta_title_about: About the Plan – Komaki East School Reorganization
meta_title_voices: Community Voices – Komaki East School Reorganization
meta_title_council: Council Proceedings – Komaki East School Reorganization
meta_title_map: School Locations (Map) – Komaki East School Reorganization
meta_desc_index: A community info site providing easy-to-understand information on the Komaki East (Shinooka) school reorganization plan, planned for FY2027, based on official sources.
meta_desc_about: Detailed information on the plan to reorganize 5 elementary and 3 junior high schools into 4 schools in FY2027 — background, content, and commuting districts.
meta_desc_schedule: A summary of the reorganization plan schedule, including briefings, opinion exchanges, and planned implementation dates.
meta_desc_faq: Frequently asked questions and answers about the school reorganization plan, covering commuting, facilities, schedule, and more.
meta_desc_voices: A collection of pro, con, and neutral voices from residents, parents, and experts on the school reorganization plan.
meta_desc_council: A clear summary of council proceedings at the 1st Regular Session of 2026 (March), including the school ordinance amendment and Shinooka reorganization deliberations.
meta_desc_map: View the locations of 8 current schools and 4 post-reorganization schools in the Shinooka area on Google Maps, with East/West group merger destinations.
meta_title_bus: School Bus Plan – Komaki East School Reorganization
meta_desc_bus: Details on the Shinooka district school bus plan launching in FY2027. Service areas, schedules, costs, safety measures, and FAQ.
bus_p: School buses will be introduced for elementary school students who will face longer commutes due to the school reorganization starting in FY2027 (April 2027). The city covers all costs — students ride free.
bus_stats_intro: These three points form the backbone of the school bus plan. See the sections below for details on service areas, safety measures, and registration.
bus_ov_day_p: Operates on school days. Service is suspended during long vacations but runs on school event days such as sports day and term start days.
bus_ov_trips_p:
Morning: 1 trip (all grades)
Afternoon: up to 3 trips (lower grades / upper grades / after extracurricular activities)
bus_ov_free_p: Free for students. The city covers all costs in consideration of the longer commutes resulting from the reorganization.
bus_ov_apply_p: Students in the service area register to use the bus. Capacity and group assignments are reviewed each school year.
bus_area_criteria_p: Children with a one-way commute of 2 km or more, and areas where local conditions such as significant elevation changes apply, are eligible.
bus_area_east_p: Ikenouchi, Hayashi, Noguchi, Oyama, and Okusa districts
bus_area_outside: ※ Children outside the service area may be recommended by the Board of Education to use the bus if they are significantly far from the nearest school walking group and group commuting is extremely difficult.
bus_area_pending: ⚠️ Bus stop locations, routes, and timetables will be determined in the forthcoming School Bus Operation Guidelines. Please check the official city website for the latest information.
bus_tl1_body: Pre-enrollment experience visits. Activities to help children familiarize themselves with their new schools.
bus_tl2_body: Trial rides and test operations. Routes and bus stops will be confirmed step by step.
bus_tl3_body: Full service launch alongside Phase 1 reorganization. Shinooka area school buses begin full operations.
bus_safety_check_p: A system will be introduced to allow parents and schools to confirm in real time that their child has boarded the bus.
bus_safety_left_p: A system will be installed to prevent children from being left behind on the bus, ensuring safety after boarding.
bus_safety_guide_p: A trained bus escort, contracted by the operator, will ride with students. Escorts receive pre-service training based on established guidelines.
bus_faq_q1: BoardingWhat if my child misses the bus?
bus_faq_a1: Procedures for missed buses will be set out in the forthcoming School Bus Operation Guidelines. Details will be communicated once finalized.
bus_faq_q2: EmergenciesWhat happens during typhoons or weather warnings?
bus_faq_a2: Procedures for alert days will be defined in the guidelines and coordinated with the school's attendance decisions.
bus_faq_q3: AreaCan children outside the service area ride the bus?
bus_faq_a3: In principle, only students in the service area are eligible. However, if a child is significantly far from the nearest walking group and group commuting is extremely difficult, the Board of Education may recommend bus use (per Q&A from the 5th Community Meeting).
bus_faq_a4: Yes. The city covers all costs because the reorganization increases commute distances for these students. There is no charge for students or families.
bus_faq_q5: Bus StopsWhere will the bus stops be?
bus_faq_a5: Specific bus stop locations will be published in the guidelines following consultations with schools and operators. The city will announce them once decided.
bus_faq_q6: RegistrationWhen and how do we apply?
bus_faq_a6: The timing and process for registration are still being finalized. The Board of Education will contact eligible families directly. Check the official city website for the latest updates.
comm_def_p1: A Community Council is an organization voluntarily formed by local residents based on the elementary school district. Groups such as neighborhood associations, PTAs, senior clubs, sports clubs, and welfare commissioners participate across organizational lines to discuss local issues and revitalize community life.
comm_def_p2: In Komaki City, Community Councils have been established in 14 of the city's 16 elementary school districts (as of 2025). The five elementary school districts in the Shinooka area (Shinooka, Hikari-ga-oka, Daishiro, Momo-ga-oka, and Sue) each have their own council.
comm_act_intro: Community Councils carry out a wide range of activities that span generations.
comm_act_list:
• Disaster preparedness & safety: Organizing and participating in area-wide disaster drills
  • Children's events: Kite-flying, mochi-pounding, walk rallies, sports meets, and more
  • Community exchange: Summer festivals, fireworks shows, music festivals
  • Senior support: Wellness salons and Orange Cafés (dementia cafés)
  • Digital support: Smartphone classes
comm_base_p: As the name "○○ Elementary School District" implies, the elementary school district is the geographic foundation of each Community Council. School districts serve as units of social cohesion, and Community Councils use this framework to cultivate horizontal connections among residents.
comm_cs_p1: Each school in Komaki City has a School Management Council (Community School). This is a forum where parents and local residents can share opinions on the school's educational policies and operations — an important institution connecting Community Councils with schools.
comm_cs_p2: It creates opportunities for local adults to participate in classroom support, read-alouds, and career education, and to share school challenges with the wider community.
comm_fac_p: The gymnasiums, sports fields, and pools of each school are used daily by local residents for community sports and as disaster bases (evacuation sites). Community members also participate in school events such as sports meets and learning fairs, making schools serve as the public "face" of the community.
comm_club_p: After-school childcare clubs for children of dual-income families are also organized on a school-district basis and operate in coordination with Community Councils and PTAs.
comm_row1_after: To be reorganized into 2 councils; transition to new structure planned for FY2029. Specific groupings are currently under review.
comm_row2_after: Will be newly organized in each of the 4 new schools after reorganization. (From official Komaki City Q&A, Q11)
comm_row3_after: Even after consolidation, gymnasiums and sports grounds of closed schools will in principle be retained as "important community bases for local sports and disaster preparedness."
comm_row4_after: Placement by school district will continue.
comm_row5_after: Consolidation will expand the activity area. Continuity of existing events and activities will be decided through discussions within each council and community.
comm_qa1_q: What will happen to current activities when the councils are reduced to two?
comm_qa1_a: The city has announced a policy of reorganizing to 2 Community Councils by FY2029, but how the integration proceeds and how each event will be handled will be determined through consultations with residents and the individual councils. At this point, many details about how activities will be handed over remain undecided, and it is hoped that a forum will be provided for residents' voices to be reflected.
comm_qa2_q: Won't community bonds weaken if the local school disappears?
comm_qa2_a: Schools have served as hubs for community events, disaster preparedness, sports, and childcare. School consolidation means the number of these hubs will change, but the city has declared a policy of continuing community use of closed school facilities, so gymnasiums and sports fields are expected to remain available. How to rebuild the community itself will need to be discussed with residents taking the lead.
comm_qa3_q: Will Community School activities disappear with the school consolidation?
comm_qa3_a: They won't disappear. New School Management Councils (Community Schools) will be organized in each of the 4 new schools after reorganization (Tōkadai East Elementary, West Elementary, East Junior High, West Junior High). However, how to carry on the relationships and activities cultivated by each current school's Community School depends on future consultations.
comm_qa4_q: What will happen to local events that each community has independently continued?
comm_qa4_a: Events that each Community Council has organized over many years (disaster drills, children's events, local festivals, etc.) are activities carried out by local residents. Even after the councils are integrated, continuation is possible based on the will and consensus of local residents, but the specific form will be at the discretion of the new post-reorganization councils. No official policy has yet been announced.
